1: The speech introduction formula following immediately after ch.14's death sentence is theologically significant. The supplemental offering laws (ch.15) presuppose that Israel will enter the land ('when you come into the land,' v2) — a statement of hope immediately after the Kadesh judgment. This juxtaposition is deliberate: even as YHWH sentences the current generation, he speaks to the next generation about their future in the land. The law is addressed to those who will enter, not those who will die in the wilderness.
2: 'When you come into the land you are to inhabit, which I am giving you' — the conditional framing assumes successful settlement. Laws 'when you come into the land' are a distinct genre in the Pentateuch, given in anticipation of conditions that will exist only after the conquest (cf. Deut 12:1ff). Their placement here, after the Kadesh judgment, may serve to reorient Israel's attention from the closed door of the immediate past toward the open door of the distant future — the land promise is still operative for the next generation.
3: The category of fire offerings (isheh) offered 'as a burnt offering, or to fulfill a vow, or as a freewill offering, or at your appointed festivals' encompasses the full range of voluntary and obligatory sacrifices. The inclusion of freewill offerings (nedavah) alongside vow-fulfillment offerings and festival sacrifices shows that supplemental grain and wine provisions apply across all sacrifice types, not just the mandatory ones. The ANE context: Ugaritic ritual texts (KTU 1.40, 1.119) contain similar graduated sacrifice lists specifying grain and oil proportions by animal type, and the Ugaritic sacrificial tariff structure parallels the Numbers 15 formulas closely.
4: The grain offering proportions for a lamb: one-tenth of an ephah (isaron) of fine flour mixed with one-quarter of a hin of oil, plus a quarter-hin of wine for the drink offering. These specific volumetric measures (isaron, hin) are standardized Levitical units. An ephah was approximately 22 liters; a hin was approximately 3.7 liters. The standardization of these proportions across all sacrificial occasions ensures consistency in the sanctuary's cultic economy regardless of which festival or offering type is being observed.
5: A quarter-hin of wine for the drink offering accompanying the lamb. The inclusion of wine in the sacrificial system is noteworthy: wine was among the most valuable agricultural products in the ancient Levant, and wine offerings to deities are well-attested in Canaanite (Ugaritic), Egyptian, and Mesopotamian cult. The Israelite drink offering (nesekh) is probably derived from the wider ANE practice of libation offerings poured out before or upon the altar.
6: For a ram: two-tenths of an ephah of flour with one-third of a hin of oil. The graduated scale (larger animal = larger grain accompaniment) mirrors the economic logic of the graduated chatat scale (Lev 5:6-11) and reflects a general ANE principle that offerings should be proportional to the value of the main sacrifice. Ugaritic sacrificial tariffs (RS 24.643) contain similar graduated grain proportions for different animal classes.
7: One-third of a hin of wine for a ram. The drink offering quantity scales proportionally with the flour and oil measures — maintaining consistent ratios across animal sizes. This mathematical proportionality is a hallmark of priestly legislation in the Pentateuch and reflects the administrative sophistication of the Levitical system.
8: For a bull (the largest standard sacrificial animal): three-tenths of an ephah of flour with half a hin of oil. The tripling of the minimum quantity (3/10 vs. 1/10 for a lamb) for the largest animal maintains the proportional structure. Bulls were the most expensive sacrificial animals and were used primarily in the most significant offerings (burnt offerings, sin offerings, dedication ceremonies), so the larger grain-accompaniment investment was appropriate.
9: Three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour with half a hin of oil — the full grain offering accompanying a bull. The precision of these measures in the priestly legislation is consistent with what we know of ancient temple economies: Mesopotamian temple archives (especially from Ur III and neo-Babylonian periods) kept meticulous records of grain, oil, and wine allocations for cultic use, down to fractional measures.
10: Half a hin of wine for a bull. The complete scale — lamb (1/4 hin), ram (1/3 hin), bull (1/2 hin) — creates a proportional structure that can be memorized and applied consistently. The pleasing aroma (rei'ach nicho'ach) formula that closes the wine offering instructions is the standard descriptor for sacrifices accepted by YHWH, used over 40 times in the Pentateuch.
11: 'This shall be done for each bull, each ram, and each lamb or goat.' The universal application clause ensures the supplemental offering system applies regardless of the sacrifice's purpose — there is no category of animal offering that bypasses the grain-oil-wine accompaniment requirement.
12: Proportional scaling for multiple animals: whatever the number, each animal receives its proportional grain offering. The clause ensures that a person offering ten rams does not bring ten times the single-ram measure — each individual offering maintains its complete grain accompaniment. This individualization of the supplemental offerings prevents either under-providing or over-providing for the sanctuary stores.
13: 'Every native Israelite shall follow this procedure' — the term ezrach (native/citizen, literally 'one who grows here,' from zarach, to rise/grow) is the standard term for the indigenous Israelite in legal contexts. The specification of native Israelite procedure before extending it to resident aliens (v14) establishes the baseline law that will then be universalized.
14: The extension to resident aliens (ger) who present offerings — they follow the same procedure as native Israelites. The ger (sojourner/resident alien) was a significant social category in ancient Israel — a non-Israelite who lived within the community without full tribal membership. The Pentateuch consistently extends religious and civil protections to the ger (Exod 12:48-49; Lev 19:33-34), and here the extension covers cultic participation: the ger who wishes to offer sacrifices does so by the same rules as a native citizen.
15: 'One statute for the congregation (chukkat olam, permanent statute) — for you and for the resident alien' — the universalization principle. No second-class liturgical participation for resident aliens: the same rules, the same grain offerings, the same proportions. The phrase chukkat olam (permanent statute) signals this is not a temporary accommodation but a permanent feature of the covenant community's structure. This inclusion of non-Israelites under the same sacrificial law anticipates the NT's breaking down of the Jew/Gentile distinction in worship (Eph 2:14-16).
16: 'One law and one rule (mishpat echad) shall apply to both you and the resident alien.' The restatement emphasizes the legal equality in cultic matters — not just a pious aspiration but a statutory requirement. The mishpat (law/judgment/rule) vocabulary makes this a legal, not merely ethical, equality.
17: A new divine speech introducing the firstfruits-of-dough law (vv.18-21). The separate speech introduction signals a distinct legal unit from the grain-offering proportions of vv.3-16.
18: 'When you come into the land to which I am bringing you' — the land-entry framing recurs from v2, reinforcing the forward-looking orientation of ch.15 as legislation for the next generation. The firstfruits laws are all grounded in the agricultural economy that Israel will have in the land — the wilderness has no such economy.
19: The firstfruits contribution (terumah) from the land's produce is the general principle: 'you shall present a contribution (terumah) as a gift to the LORD.' The terumah (heave offering/contribution) is lifted up (rum) as a dedication to YHWH's portion — it is distinguished from the tithe and the firstfruits in technical detail but shares the principle of the land's yield belonging first to YHWH.
20: 'From the first of your dough (reshit arisotekhem) you shall present a loaf as a contribution.' The firstfruits of the bread-making process — specifically the first batch of dough prepared from the new grain — was to be offered before the household consumed any. This law is the basis for the Jewish practice of challah (taking a portion of dough before baking, which Jews still practice today as a remembrance of this command). The specific focus on processed grain (dough rather than raw grain) distinguishes this from the harvest firstfruits laws elsewhere.
21: 'Throughout your generations' (ledoroteichem) — the challah-dough offering is a permanent generational obligation, not limited to the first harvest. The continuity of the obligation across generations serves as a repeated acknowledgment that the land and its produce belong ultimately to YHWH, who gave it to Israel as a gift.
22: The unintentional sin provisions begin: 'But if you sin unintentionally (bishegagah) and do not observe all these commandments.' The category of shegagah (unintentional/inadvertent sin, literally 'erring/straying') is distinct from the high-handed sin of v30. The difference is moral awareness: unintentional sins are committed without full awareness of their transgression; high-handed sins are committed with deliberate defiance.
23: The scope of the unintentional sin provision covers 'all that the LORD commanded through Moses from the day the LORD gave the command and onward throughout your generations' — the entire Mosaic corpus, not just the supplemental offerings of this chapter. The provision applies to inadvertent violations of any commandment in any generation.
24: If the unintentional sin was committed by the congregation as a whole without their knowledge, the atonement requires a bull as burnt offering with its grain/drink accompaniments, plus a goat as sin offering — the standard communal sin offering package. The combination of an 'olah (expressing dedication) and a chattat (expressing purification) addresses both the need for renewed consecration and the need for cleansing from inadvertent ritual transgression.
25: The priest makes atonement for the whole congregation, and they 'shall be forgiven, for it was unintentional (shegagah).' The causal connection between the sacrifice's performance and the forgiveness is direct: venislach lahem (and they shall be forgiven). The Levitical atonement system is presented as reliably effective for inadvertent sins — the mechanism functions as designed when the categories are correctly applied.
26: The forgiveness extends to 'the resident alien living among them' — the same universalization seen in vv.15-16. The communal atonement covers the entire resident population, not just native Israelites. This means the ger shares both the cultic obligation (v13-14) and the cultic benefit (forgiveness) of the congregational atonement system.
27: The individual (rather than congregational) unintentional sin offering: one yearling female goat (seir izzim bat shenata). The provision of an individual offering for inadvertent sin parallels the graduated chatat of Lev 5:1-13 (which includes poverty provisions). The fact that ch.15 does not include poverty provisions suggests this is a supplemental specification, not a replacement of the Lev 5 system.
28: The priest makes atonement for the individual, 'making atonement before the LORD for the person who sinned' — and the person 'shall be forgiven.' The formula is identical to v25's communal atonement — same mechanism, same result, scaled to an individual. The priestly mediation system is designed to handle both corporate and individual sin under the same theological framework.
29: 'You shall have one law for the person who acts unintentionally, whether native-born among the Israelites or a resident alien.' The universalization formula applied to individual atonement: whether Israelite or ger, the procedure and the forgiveness are identical. This legal equality in atonement access is notable — in ANE temple systems, access to priestly atonement was typically restricted to citizens of the city or nation, not to resident foreigners.
30: 'But the person who acts defiantly (beyad ramah, literally with a raised hand)' — the high-handed sin category. The raised-hand gesture is a posture of defiance or challenge in both ancient and modern idiom. This person 'blasphemes the LORD' (megadef et YHWH) — the deliberate, knowing transgression of a divine command is treated as an act of contempt for YHWH's authority rather than mere inadvertence. The death sentence (v35) and cutting off from the people (nikretah, karet) apply to this category. There is no sacrificial remedy for high-handed sin in the Levitical system.
31: 'Because he has despised the word of the LORD (ki devar YHWH bazah)' — the precise characterization: high-handed sin is the despising (bazah, to hold in contempt) of YHWH's word. This root appears in Saul's sin (1 Sam 15:9, when he spares Agag: YHWH says Saul has rejected YHWH's word) and David's sin (2 Sam 12:9-10: 'You have despised the word of the LORD'). The karet penalty (being 'cut off' from the people) is the covenant sanction that removes the sinner from the community — whether by human execution, premature death, or childlessness is debated, but the effect is exclusion from Israel's covenant life and legacy.
32: The Sabbath wood-gatherer case immediately follows the high-handed sin legislation as an illustration. The placement is interpretively significant: the case was apparently debated as to whether it was inadvertent or high-handed. The man is 'found' (vayimtse'u) gathering wood — the Hebrew suggests he was caught in the act by community members, not that he reported himself. The Sabbath prohibition on labor was one of the most fundamental covenant obligations, explicitly carrying the death penalty (Exod 31:14-15; 35:2).
33: The wood-gatherer is brought before Moses, Aaron, and the whole congregation. The formal assembly of the community for the legal case follows the pattern of communal judicial procedure in the ancient Levant: cases involving capital punishment required the full community's deliberation and execution. The bringing of the case to Moses and Aaron reflects the still-developing state of Israel's legal system — not every application of divine law had been worked out in advance.
34: 'They put him in custody because it had not been made clear (lo forash) what should be done to him.' This remarkable admission of legal uncertainty in the narrative acknowledges that not every case was pre-adjudicated by the existing laws. The technical vocabulary lo forash (had not been specified/declared) is a legal term for a gap in existing legislation — a lacuna that requires divine clarification. This consultation mechanism (detaining until YHWH speaks) parallels the later use of Urim and Thummim for legal uncertainty (Num 27:21).
35: YHWH's clarification: the death penalty, executed by stoning outside the camp with the whole congregation participating. The outside-the-camp execution location reflects the same logic as the burning of sin offering remains outside the camp (Lev 4:12,21) — what is under divine judgment or laden with impurity must be removed from the sacred space of the covenant community. The communal execution ('the whole congregation shall stone him') distributes the responsibility across the entire community, preventing the emergence of an executioner class and ensuring corporate accountability for covenant enforcement.
36: The congregation executes the sentence exactly as commanded. The compliance formula ('as the LORD commanded Moses') confirms that the wood-gatherer incident is a case-law illustration of the high-handed sin principle: gathering wood on the Sabbath was deliberately defiant, not inadvertent. The incident thus closes the gap created by vv.30-31 — the abstract principle now has a concrete, narrated application.
37: A new divine speech introduces the tzitzit (fringe/tassel) law. The placement of the tzitzit command at the end of ch.15 — after the inadvertent-sin provision, the high-handed-sin ruling, and the wood-gatherer case — is pedagogically significant: the tzitzit are introduced as a memory device to prevent both inadvertent sin (by reminding one of all the commandments) and high-handed sin (by keeping the commandments constantly before one's eyes).
38: The tzitzit command: tassels on the corners (kanfei vegadeihem, the wings/corners of their garments) with a blue cord (ptil tekhelet). The tekhelet blue was made from the hyacinth murex snail (Hexaplex trunculus, or possibly Murex brandaris) found on the Mediterranean coast. The dyeing process was expensive and time-consuming — the same dye used for royal and priestly garments across the ancient world. The blue color was associated with divinity and royalty in Egypt (pharaonic blue), Mesopotamia (lapis lazuli as divine color), and Ugarit (divine garments described as blue). The command to put blue on every Israelite's garment democratizes a color previously restricted to the elite — every covenant member wears the royal/divine color as a mark of their status before YHWH.
39: 'The tassels shall be for you to look at, so that you may remember all the LORD's commandments.' The tzitzit function as a visual mnemonic — a covenant memory device worn on the body. This is comparable to other ancient mnemonic and apotropaic practices: Egyptian menat necklaces, Mesopotamian amulets inscribed with divine names, and Israelite tefillin (phylacteries) all serve the function of keeping the wearer in relation to the sacred. The rabbinic tradition counts the numerical value of tzitzit (600) plus 8 threads plus 5 knots = 613, corresponding to the traditional count of Torah commandments — the entire Torah encoded in the garment's fringe. The danger being addressed is 'not following your own heart and eyes' (lo taturu) — independent moral reasoning that strays from the covenant.
40: 'So you shall remember and do all my commandments, and you shall be holy to your God.' The tzitzit law closes with the holiness imperative that opened the Holiness Code (Lev 19:2). The connection between remembering-doing and being holy is direct: the tzitzit are a behavioral reminder system whose purpose is covenant fidelity, whose goal is holiness. The visual cue → memory → action → holiness chain summarizes the pedagogical logic of the command.
41: 'I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God.' The chapter closes with the foundational self-identification formula — the same formula that opens the Ten Commandments (Exod 20:2). The tzitzit law and the entire supplemental legislation of ch.15 are grounded in and conclude with the Exodus-identity statement: YHWH's authority to command is inseparable from his identity as Israel's Redeemer. The 'I am the LORD your God' formula grounds every subsequent obligation in the prior act of redemptive grace — the imperative always rests upon the indicative of divine deliverance.
